file_name: S et al. - 2025 - Detection of skin cancer using random forest algorithm in python.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper explicitly uses the Random Forest machine learning algorithm to classify skin cancer (melanoma) from dermoscopic images and clinical data. It details the extraction of features such as ABCD rule descriptors, GLCM texture, and shape descriptors, and reports performance metrics including accuracy (76.87% to 98%), sensitivity, and specificity for distinguishing benign from malignant lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the application of the Random Forest algorithm to detect skin cancer. It details specific methodology including data collection from Kaggle and ISIC datasets, preprocessing steps (watershed segmentation, feature extraction of ABCD rule, GLCM, and shape descriptors), and model training/testing (80/20 split, 10-fold cross-validation). It reports original performance metrics such as accuracy (76.87% to 98%), sensitivity, and specificity, which characterizes it as a technical study producing new results rather than a literature review.

title: Detection of Skin Cancer Using Random Forest Algorithm in Python
journal: International Scientific Journal of Engineering and Management
brief_summary: This study evaluates the Random Forest algorithm for detecting melanoma skin cancer using dermoscopic images and clinical data sourced from Kaggle and the ISIC dataset. The methodology involved preprocessing 3,297 images via watershed segmentation and extracting features based on the ABCD rule, Grey Level Co-occurrence Matrix, and shape descriptors. The dataset was split into 80% for training and 20% for testing, with performance assessed using 10-fold cross-validation on 1,000 ISIC images. The Random Forest classifier achieved an accuracy of 76.87%, sensitivity of 78.43%, and specificity of 75.31% using ABCD features, while also demonstrating high robustness with raw color features. Additionally, the model reported an overall accuracy of 0.98 on a separate structured dataset analysis, confirming its effectiveness in distinguishing benign from malignant lesions.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary focus of the study. The dataset contained 1,497 malignant lesions out of 3,297 total images. The model aimed to distinguish malignant cases (class 1) from benign ones.

Used as the negative class for binary classification. The dataset contained 1,800 benign lesions out of 3,297 total images. The model achieved high precision/recall for class 0 (benign) in the confusion matrix analysis.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic label: Benign (class 0) vs. Malignant/Melanoma (class 1)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is to 'classify skin cancer' and 'distinguish between benign and malignant lesions'. The target variable is binary ('Skin Cancer' where 1 indicates presence and 0 indicates absence). The model uses Random Forest to predict discrete classes based on extracted features (ABCD rule, GLCM, shape descriptors) from dermoscopic images and clinical data. Performance is reported using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, recall, and F-score for class 0 and class 1.

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Skin Cancer Dataset
datasets_used.description: A structured dataset containing clinical and visual features (age, gender, skin type, sun exposure, family history, number of moles, symptoms, asymmetry, border irregularity, color changes, diameter, evolution) sourced from Kaggle. It includes a target variable 'Skin Cancer' (1 for presence, 0 for absence).

use.training: Used for training the Random Forest classifier on clinical and visual features.
use.testing: Used for testing the model, achieving an overall accuracy of 0.98.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Dataset (Subset)
datasets_used.description: A subset of the International Skin Imaging Collaboration dataset comprising 3,297 dermoscopic images with expert annotations. It includes 1,800 benign and 1,497 malignant lesions. Features were extracted using watershed segmentation, ABCD rule descriptors, GLCM texture, and shape descriptors.

use.training: Approximately 80% (2,637 images) was used for training the Random Forest model.
use.testing: The remaining 20% (660 images) was used for testing. Additionally, 10-fold cross-validation was performed on 1,000 ISIC images for performance analysis.
use.other: Used for feature extraction (ABCD, GLCM, shape) and validation via 10-fold cross-validation on 1,000 images.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, cited as a well-documented dataset used in the field for stratified k-fold validation.

use.other: Cited as a reference dataset for stratified k-fold validation in related studies (Fernandes et al., 2023).

datasets_used.name: BCN20000
datasets_used.description: A dataset consisting of 19,424 dermoscopic images ranging from difficult cases such as mucosal and subungual lesions, acquired during 2010–2016. Cited as a publicly accessible repository.

use.other: Cited as a publicly accessible repository for dermoscopic images.

datasets_used.name: PAD‑UFES‑20
datasets_used.description: A dataset consisting of 2,298 smartphone-acquired images from 1,641 lesions and 1,373 patients, with 58.4% biopsy-proven. Cited for general clinical use.

use.other: Cited as a dataset for general clinical use.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the performance of the Random Forest classifier on 1,000 ISIC images.
To evaluate classification outcomes, including true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives, and to calculate error rates.

statistical_methods_used.method: Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision, Recall, F-score
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To quantify the diagnostic performance of the Random Forest algorithm in distinguishing between benign and malignant lesions.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, recall, and F-score via confusion matrix analysis and 10-fold cross-validation.

ISIC Dataset (Dermoscopic Images).description: Dataset of 3,297 dermoscopic images (1,800 benign, 1,497 malignant). 80% used for training, 20% for testing. 10-fold cross-validation performed on 1,000 ISIC images.

Random Forest (Raw Colour Features).notes: Highest accuracy and F-score among tested classifiers (SVM, Naive Bayes, AdaBoost, Bayesian Network) for skin detection using raw colour features.

ISIC Dataset (Dermoscopic Images).notes: SVM showed superior performance in some comparisons against Random Forest on this dataset.

Kaggle Skin Cancer Dataset (Structured/C.description: Structured dataset with clinical and visual features. Total 10,001 samples (8,000 training, 2,001 testing). Confusion matrix analysis reported on 2,000 samples (872 benign, 1,128 malignant).
Kaggle Skin Cancer Dataset (Structured/C.notes: Model achieved macro and weighted averages of 1.00 for precision, recall, and F-score. Only 4 misclassified samples out of 2,000 considered in the confusion matrix.

Grey Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) for texture
Clinical features (age, gender, skin type, sun exposure, family history, number of moles, symptoms, diameter, evolution)

preprocessing_techniques: Watershed segmentation for lesion isolation from dermoscopic images
Handling of missing or inconsistent values (filling null values, removing unnecessary/irrelevant data entries)
One-hot encoding for categorical variables (e.g., gender, skin type)
Normalization of numerical variables to create a uniform scale
Outlier detection to remove extreme observed values
Feature extraction of ABCD rule descriptors (Asymmetry, Border, Colour, Diameter)
Feature extraction using Grey Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) for texture
conclusions: The Random Forest algorithm demonstrated superior predictive performance for identifying skin cancer cases compared to other tested machine learning algorithms, including Support Vector Machine (SVM), K-Nearest Neighbours (KNN), and Logistic Regression.

Random Forest achieved an overall accuracy of 0.98 (98%) on a structured dataset of 10,001 samples, with a very low error rate of 0.2% and perfect precision, recall, and F1-scores (1.00) for both benign and malignant classes in the confusion matrix analysis.
When applied to dermoscopic images from the ISIC dataset using ABCD rule features, Random Forest achieved an accuracy of 76.87%, sensitivity of 78.43%, and specificity of 75.31%.
Random Forest exhibited robustness and effectiveness in skin detection using raw colour features, achieving the highest accuracy (87.7%) and F-score (0.739) among all tested classifiers.

The ensemble learning nature of Random Forest, which builds multiple decision trees, helps avoid overfitting and allows it to effectively extract complexity from non-linear patterns and interactions in clinical features such as age, number of moles, asymmetry, and color.

Random Forest is considered the most reliable and efficient model for predicting melanoma skin cancer from structured datasets due to its robustness, high accuracy, and ability to handle complex data interactions better than linear models like Logistic Regression or parameter-sensitive models like SVM.
important_remarks: The paper reports an unusually high accuracy of 98% (0.98) with perfect precision, recall, and F1-scores (1.00) for both classes on the Kaggle structured dataset, which raises concerns about potential data leakage or overfitting given the simplistic nature of the features compared to complex dermoscopic images.

There is a discrepancy in the reported testing sample size for the ISIC dataset: the abstract and methodology state 10-fold cross-validation on 1,000 images, while the dataset description lists 660 testing samples (20% of 3,297), creating ambiguity about the true evaluation scope.

The study relies on a small, non-standard subset of the ISIC dataset (3,297 images) rather than the full HAM10000 or ISIC archives, limiting the generalizability of the dermoscopic image classification results (76.87% accuracy) to broader clinical populations.

The paper does not report any statistical significance tests (p-values) or confidence intervals for the performance metrics, making it impossible to assess the statistical robustness of the differences between Random Forest and other classifiers like SVM.

The comparison with deep learning models (CNNs) is based on external citations (e.g., Kumar et al., 2022) rather than direct experimental comparison within this study, weakening the claim that Random Forest is superior to CNNs for this specific task.

The dataset composition is imbalanced (58.60% malignant cases in the abstract vs. 54.3% in the detailed breakdown), and while the Kaggle dataset shows perfect classification, the ISIC subset results show lower sensitivity (78.43%) and specificity (75.31%), suggesting potential bias or difficulty in generalizing to real-world dermoscopic variability.
